items.title: Peripheral vs central
items.contentHtml:
Peripheral vs central classification

  • Peripheral facial palsy (lesion of the facial nerve or its nucleus): affects the entire ipsilateral hemifacies, including the frontalis (drooping eyebrow, inability to wrinkle the forehead) and orbicularis oculi.
  • Central facial palsy (supranuclear lesion, corticonuclear pathway): spares the upper motor neuron of the facial nucleus (bilateral innervation frontalis), so the patient may wrinkle his forehead; weakness predominates in the contralateral lower quadrant.
  • Key fact: the descended corner of the mouth sign is nonspecific; Differentiation is made by evaluating the frontalis muscle and the strength of the orbicularis oculi.

items.foco: Facial motor nucleus and its outputs
items.title: facial motor nucleus
items.contentHtml:
Facial motor nucleus and its outputs

  • The motor nucleus of the facial nerve is located in the pons (pons), in the pontine tegmentum ventrolateral to the abducens nucleus.
  • Its outputs: brachymotor fibers that innervate the muscles of facial mimicry, the stapedius (stapedial branch), the posterior belly of the digastric muscle and the stylohyoid.
  • The superior portion of the facial nucleus (frontalis and orbicularis oculi) receives bilateral corticonuclear innervation; the lower portion, only contralateral. This explains the frontal preservation in central palsy.

items.title: Bell's palsy
items.contentHtml:
Bell's palsy: favorable prognosis

  • Bell's palsy is an idiopathic peripheral facial paralysis, with acute onset (<72 hours), with exclusion of other causes (infectious, neoplastic, vascular).
  • Favorable prognosis: 70% of patients recover full function; recovery begins in the first 3 weeks.
  • Poor prognostic factors: complete paralysis at the beginning, age >60 years, high blood pressure, loss of taste, hyperacusis, and absence of action potentials in electromyography at 10-14 days.

items.title: Ramsay Hunt
items.contentHtml:
Ramsay Hunt syndrome: vesicles

  • Ramsay Hunt syndrome is the reactivation of the varicella-zoster virus in the geniculate ganglion, causing ipsilateral peripheral facial paralysis + herpetic vesicles in the pinna, external auditory canal and/or oral mucosa.
  • It can be accompanied by intense otalgia, hearing loss, vertigo and alteration of taste. in the anterior two thirds of the tongue.
  • First-line treatment is acyclovir 800 mg 5 times a day for 7 days (or valacyclovir 1 g every 8 hours) associated with prednisone 60 mg/day with progressive reduction in 7-10 days. The prognosis is worse than in Bell's palsy.

items.foco: Involvement of the greater superficial petrosal nerve
items.title: Greater superficial petrosal nerve
items.contentHtml:
Involvement of the greater superficial petrosal nerve

  • The greater superficial petrosal nerve is a branch of the facial nerve that emerges from the geniculate ganglion and carries preganglionic parasympathetic fibers to the pterygopalatine ganglion.
  • Its function: secretomotor innervation of the lacrimal gland, nasal and palatine glands.
  • The involvement proximal to the exit of the superficial petrosal nerve major (lesion in the facial canal before the geniculate ganglion) produces hypolacrimia (dry eye) ipsilateral, detectable with the Schirmer test (<10 mm at 5 minutes).

items.foco: Sign of the lowered corner of the mouth
items.title: Lowered corner of the mouth
items.contentHtml:
Depressed oral commissure sign

  • The descended oral commissure sign indicates weakness of the depressor anguli oris muscle (platysma and depressor anguli oris).
  • It is a non-specific finding that can appear in both peripheral and central facial paralysis, which is why it does not allow us to differentiate the level of the lesion.
  • To distinguish, the ability to forehead wrinkling (frontal function, bilaterally innervated): if preserved, suggests central paralysis; If it is abolished, it suggests peripheral paralysis.

items.title: central stroke
items.contentHtml:
Cause: central stroke

  • The cerebrovascular accident (ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke) that affects the corticonuclear pathway (facial motor cortex or geniculate tract) produces contralateral central facial paralysis.
  • Key data: it spares the frontalis (the patient can wrinkle the forehead), the weakness is more evident in the lower lip and the corner of the mouth, and is usually associated with Ipsilateral hemiparesis of the upper and lower extremity.
  • non-contrast head computed tomography is the first image in suspected acute stroke to rule out hemorrhage; MRI with diffusion is more sensitive for early ischemia.

items.title: Corneal reflex
items.contentHtml:
Corneal reflex and its alteration

  • The corneal reflex is a trigemino-facial reflex: the input travels through the ophthalmic branch of the trigeminal nerve (V1) to the spinal trigeminal nucleus, and the motor output exits through the facial nerve (VII) to contract the orbicularis oculi.
  • In peripheral facial paralysis, the Corneal reflex is absent or diminished ipsilaterally when stimulating the cornea on the same side, because the output is damaged.
  • In central facial palsy, the corneal reflex is preserved (facial output is intact, although the voluntary pathway is affected).

items.title: Prognostic electromyography
items.contentHtml:
Electromyography for prognosis

  • Electromyography (EMG) and nerve conduction studies are performed between 10 and 14 days after the onset of paralysis to evaluate the degree of denervation and reinnervation potential.
  • Favorable finding: presence of compound muscle action potentials (CMAP) with amplitude >30% of the side healthy, which predicts good recovery in 3-6 months.
  • Unfavorable finding: absence of CMAP or amplitude <10% of the healthy side, which suggests severe denervation and high probability of incomplete recovery with sequelae (synkinesis, contracture).

items.title: Corticosteroids and antivirals
items.contentHtml:
Treatment with corticosteroids and antivirals

  • In Bell's palsy, the first-line treatment is prednisone 60 mg/day for 5 days, followed by progressive decrease in 5 days (total 10 days). Starting within the first 72 hours significantly improves recovery.
  • The routine use of antivirals in Bell's palsy is not recommended, unless herpetic reactivation is suspected (Ramsay Hunt syndrome), where acyclovir 800 mg 5 times a day or valacyclovir 1 g every 8 hours for 7 days is associated.
  • The combination of corticosteroids + antivirals in Ramsay Hunt is superior to corticosteroids alone.

items.foco: Involvement of the chorda tympani nerve
items.title: chorda tympani
items.contentHtml:
Involvement of the chorda tympani nerve

  • The chora tympani nerve (chorda tympani) is a branch of the facial nerve that carries gustatory fibers from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue and secretomotor parasympathetic fibers to the submandibular and sublingual glands.
  • Its involvement (injury distal to the geniculate ganglion, proximal to its exit) produces ageusia (loss of taste) in the anterior two-thirds of the ipsilateral tongue and hyposalivation (dry mouth).
  • Clinical evaluation is performed with taste tests (saline, sweet) and measurement of submandibular salivary flow.

items.title: Synkinesis and contracture
items.contentHtml:
Sequelae: synkinesis and contracture

  • Syncinesis are involuntary and abnormal movements that occur during the voluntary contraction of a facial muscle group (e.g. when smiling the ipsilateral eye closes). They are a consequence of aberrant reinnervation after axonal degeneration.
  • Facial contracture is the sustained tonic contraction of the facial muscles, which gives an appearance of shortening and hemifacial rigidity.
  • Management includes rehabilitation with biofeedback exercises, botulinum toxin for synkinesis and, in severe cases, decompression surgery or muscle transfer.
